title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Stratified estimation
Formalizing this approach, suppose we have covariates in a finite discrete space Xi∈𝒳X_i\in\mathcal{X} and that treatment is random conditionally on XiX_i (i.e. we have an RCT for each group defined by XiX_i).
The assumption of conditional independence (unconfoundedness) is:
{Yi(0),Yi(1)}⟂Di|Xi=x,for all x∈𝒳
\{Y_i(0), Y_i(1)\} \perp D_i | X_i = x, \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathcal{X}

or in more general form: {Yi(0),Yi(1)}⟂Di|Xi\{Y_i(0), Y_i(1)\} \perp D_i | X_i
then the Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE) is
τ(x)=𝔼[Yi(1)−Yi(0)|Xi=x]
\tau(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) | X_i = x]

and the stratified estimator is:
τ̂STRAT=∑x∈𝒳nxnτ̂(x)
\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \frac{n_x}{n} \hat{\tau}(x)

where:
τ̂(x)=1nx,1∑{Xi=x,Di=1}Yi−1nx,0∑{Xi=x,Di=0}Yi=1nx∑{Xi=x}DiYiê(x)−(1−Di)Yi1−ê(x)
\begin{align*}
\hat{\tau}(x) & =\frac{1}{n_{x,1}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=1\}}Y_{i}-\frac{1}{n_{x,0}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=0\}}Y_{i}\\
 & =\frac{1}{n_{x}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x\}}\frac{D_{i}Y_{i}}{\hat{e}(x)}-\frac{\left(1-D_{i}\right)Y_{i}}{1-\hat{e}(x)}
\end{align*}

Notation:

nx=|{i:Xi=x}|n_x = |\{i : X_i = x\}| (number in each strata)
nx,d=|{i:Xi=x,Di=d}|n_{x,d} = |\{i : X_i = x, D_i = d\}| (number treated each strata)
ê(x)=nx,1nx\hat{e}(x) = \frac{n_{x,1}}{n_x} (estimated probability of treatment)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Theorem 2.1 (Stratified Estimation)
Central limit theorem for stratified estimator: n(τ̂STRAT−τ)⇒N(0,VSTRAT)\sqrt{n}(\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} - \tau) \Rightarrow N(0, V_{STRAT})
Asymptotic variance: VSTRAT=Var[τ(Xi)]+𝔼[σ(1)2(Xi)e(Xi)+σ(0)2(Xi)1−e(Xi)]V_{STRAT} = \text{Var}[\tau(X_i)] + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{\sigma^2_{(1)}(X_i)}{e(X_i)} + \frac{\sigma^2_{(0)}(X_i)}{1-e(X_i)}\right]
where:

e(x)=ℙ[Di=1|Xi=x]e(x) = \mathbb{P}[D_i = 1 | X_i = x] (propensity score)
σ(d)2(x)=Var[Yi(d)|Xi=x]\sigma^2_{(d)}(x) = \text{Var}[Y_i^{(d)} | X_i = x]

Decomposition of stratified estimator:
τ̂STRAT=∑x∈𝒳λ(x)τ(x)+∑x∈𝒳(λ̂(x)−λ(x))τ(x)+∑x∈𝒳λ(x)(τ̂(x)−τ(x))+∑x∈𝒳(λ̂(x)−λ(x))(τ̂(x)−τ(x))
\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \lambda(x)\tau(x) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} (\hat{\lambda}(x) - \lambda(x)) \tau(x) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \lambda(x) (\hat{\tau}(x) - \tau(x)) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} (\hat{\lambda}(x) - \lambda(x)) (\hat{\tau}(x) - \tau(x))

where:

λ(x)=ℙ[Xi=x]\lambda(x) = \mathbb{P}[X_i = x] (true probability)
λ̂(x)=nx/n\hat{\lambda}(x) = n_x/n (empirical probability)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Propensity Stratification
Sorting step: ê(Xi1)≤ê(Xi2)≤…≤ê(Xin)\hat{e}(X_{i_1}) \leq \hat{e}(X_{i_2}) \leq \ldots \leq \hat{e}(X_{i_n})
Stratum-specific estimator: τ̂j=∑j=⌊(j−1)n/J⌋+1⌊jn/J⌋DiYi∑j=⌊(j−1)n/J⌋+1⌊jn/J⌋Di−∑j=⌊(j−1)n/J⌋+1⌊jn/J⌋(1−Di)Yi∑j=⌊(j−1)n/J⌋+1⌊jn/J⌋(1−Di)\hat{\tau}_j = \frac{\sum_{j=\lfloor(j-1)n/J\rfloor+1}^{\lfloor jn/J\rfloor} D_i Y_i}{\sum_{j=\lfloor(j-1)n/J\rfloor+1}^{\lfloor jn/J\rfloor} D_i} - \frac{\sum_{j=\lfloor(j-1)n/J\rfloor+1}^{\lfloor jn/J\rfloor} (1-D_i) Y_i}{\sum_{j=\lfloor(j-1)n/J\rfloor+1}^{\lfloor jn/J\rfloor} (1-D_i)}
Propensity stratification estimator: τ̂PSTRAT=1J∑j=1Jτ̂j\hat{\tau}_{PSTRAT} = \frac{1}{J} \sum_{j=1}^J \hat{\tau}_j

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Inverse-Propensity Weighting (IPW)
IPW estimator with estimated propensity scores: τ̂IPW=1n∑i=1n(DiYiê(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−ê(Xi))\hat{\tau}_{IPW} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{D_i Y_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right)
Oracle IPW estimator (true propensity scores): τ̂IPW*=1n∑i=1n(DiYie(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−e(Xi))\hat{\tau}^*_{IPW} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{D_i Y_i}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-e(X_i)}\right)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Theorem 2.2 (Oracle IPW)
Central limit theorem for oracle IPW: n(τ̂IPW*−τ)⇒N(0,VIPW*)\sqrt{n}(\hat{\tau}^*_{IPW} - \tau) \Rightarrow N(0, V_{IPW^*})
Asymptotic variance: VIPW*=Var[τ(Xi)]+𝔼[(μ(0)(Xi)+(1−e(Xi))τ(Xi))2e(Xi)(1−e(Xi))]+𝔼[σ(1)2(Xi)e(Xi)+σ(0)2(Xi)1−e(Xi)]V_{IPW^*} = \text{Var}[\tau(X_i)] + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{(\mu_{(0)}(X_i) + (1-e(X_i))\tau(X_i))^2}{e(X_i)(1-e(X_i))}\right] + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{\sigma^2_{(1)}(X_i)}{e(X_i)} + \frac{\sigma^2_{(0)}(X_i)}{1-e(X_i)}\right]
Alternative decomposition in proof: DiYie(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−e(Xi)=τ(Xi)+(Die(Xi)−1−Di1−e(Xi))c(Xi)+Diεi(1)e(Xi)−(1−Di)εi(0)1−e(Xi)\frac{D_i Y_i}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-e(X_i)} = \tau(X_i) + \left(\frac{D_i}{e(X_i)} - \frac{1-D_i}{1-e(X_i)}\right) c(X_i) + \frac{D_i \varepsilon_i^{(1)}}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) \varepsilon_i^{(0)}}{1-e(X_i)}
where: - c(x)=μ(0)(x)+(1−e(x))τ(x)c(x) = \mu_{(0)}(x) + (1-e(x))\tau(x) - εi(w)=Yi(w)−μ(w)(Xi)\varepsilon_i^{(w)} = Y_i^{(w)} - \mu_{(w)}(X_i)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Variance Comparison
Relationship between IPW and stratified variances: VIPW*=VSTRAT+𝔼[(μ(0)(Xi)+(1−e(Xi))τ(Xi))2e(Xi)(1−e(Xi))]V_{IPW^*} = V_{STRAT} + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{(\mu_{(0)}(X_i) + (1-e(X_i))\tau(X_i))^2}{e(X_i)(1-e(X_i))}\right]
Therefore: VIPW*≥VSTRATV_{IPW^*} \geq V_{STRAT} (IPW is generally less efficient than stratification)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Overlap Assumptions
Weak overlap: 𝔼[1/(e(Xi)(1−e(Xi)))]<∞\mathbb{E}[1/(e(X_i)(1-e(X_i)))] < \infty
Strong overlap: η≤e(x)≤1−ηfor all x∈𝒳\eta \leq e(x) \leq 1-\eta \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathcal{X}
for some η>0\eta > 0.

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Auxiliary Functions and Notation
Conditional mean functions: μ(w)(x)=𝔼[Yi(w)|Xi=x]\mu^{(w)}(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y_i^{(w)} | X_i = x]
Conditional variance functions: σ(w)2(x)=Var[Yi(w)|Xi=x]\sigma^{(w)2}(x) = \text{Var}[Y_i^{(w)} | X_i = x]
Sample sizes by stratum and treatment: - nx=|{i:Xi=x}|n_x = |\{i : X_i = x\}| (total in stratum xx) - nx1=|{i:Xi=x,Di=1}|n_{x1} = |\{i : X_i = x, D_i = 1\}| (treated in stratum xx)
- nx0=|{i:Xi=x,Di=0}|n_{x0} = |\{i : X_i = x, D_i = 0\}| (control in stratum xx)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Estimation Error Terms
Decomposition of stratified estimator: τ̂STRAT=∑x∈𝒳λ(x)τ(x)+∑x∈𝒳(λ̂(x)−λ(x))τ(x)+∑x∈𝒳λ(x)(τ̂(x)−τ(x))+∑x∈𝒳(λ̂(x)−λ(x))(τ̂(x)−τ(x))\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \lambda(x)\tau(x) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} (\hat{\lambda}(x) - \lambda(x)) \tau(x) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \lambda(x) (\hat{\tau}(x) - \tau(x)) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} (\hat{\lambda}(x) - \lambda(x)) (\hat{\tau}(x) - \tau(x))
where: - λ(x)=ℙ[Xi=x]\lambda(x) = \mathbb{P}[X_i = x] (true probability) - λ̂(x)=nx/n\hat{\lambda}(x) = n_x/n (empirical probability)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text: Examples from Text

title: TwoStageDiD_draft
text:
Starting Assumptions

The True Data Generating Process
Along with continuous outcomes yigty_{igt}, our data consists of (discrete) units ii, (discrete) times tt, and (discrete) groups, indexed by time first treated gg. Under parallel trends, outcomes follow:
yigt=μg+ηt+τgtDgt+εigt(1)
\begin{align}
y_{igt} = \mu_g + \eta_t + \tau_{gt} D_{gt} + \varepsilon_{igt}
\end{align}
 \qquad(1)
Where:

μg\mu_g = group fixed effect: effects constant over all time, by group
ηt\eta_t = time fixed effect: effects constant over all groups, by time
Dgt≡𝟏g≤tD_{gt}\equiv \textbf{1}_{g\le t}: treatment indicator (note - once treated, always treated)
τgt\tau_{gt} = group-time specific treatment effect: i.e. 𝔼[Ygt1−Ygt0]\mathbb{E}[Y^1_{gt}-Y^0_{gt}] in potential outcomes notation
εigt\varepsilon_{igt} = idiosyncratic error with E[εigt]=0E[\varepsilon_{igt}] = 0

title: TwoStageDiD_draft
text:
The simplest case:
2×22\times 2 DiD: two groups, two periods (t∈{1,2}t\in\{1,2\}); one group (A, aka control ; g=∞g=\infty) never treated, the other group (B, aka treatment ; g=2g=2) treated in the second period. In the data here we have one unit per group, but in practice there will be NgtN_{gt} units in a group-time.
Under the assumptions:

parallel trends: that the untreated potential outcomes for both groups have parallel trends.
no anticipation: treatment effects are zero in all periods before treatment actually begins.

then τgt\tau_{gt} represents a causal treatment effect (the estimand).
In this case, a DiD estimator is “four means and three subtractions” (the reason this method is called diff-in-diff).


classic 2x2 example data is
# Create the classic 2x2 difference-in-differences setup
data <- data.frame(
  group = c("control", "control", "treatment", "treatment"),
  period = c(1, 2, 1, 2),
  treated = c(0, 0, 0, 1),
  # Create outcomes with known treatment effect
  outcome = c(10, 11, 12, 16)  # Treatment effect = 3
)

data$group_id <- ifelse(data$group == "control", 1, 2)
data$period_id <- data$period

data |> gt::gt() |> gtExtras::gt_theme_espn()








group
period
treated
outcome
group_id
period_id




control
1
0
10
1
1


control
2
0
11
1
2


treatment
1
0
12
2
1


treatment
2
1
16
2
2







with matrix representation:


classic 2x2 data matrix
data$outcome |> 
  matrix(nrow=2, dimnames = list(c("A","B"),c("1","2")))


   1  2
A 10 12
B 11 16


This estimator in general is calculated by taking the means of each group, subtracting the pre means from the post means of each group, and then subtracting the treated differences from the untreated differences:


classic 2x2 four means and three subtractions
# Classic DiD calculation: 
# (Y_treat_post - Y_treat_pre) - (Y_control_post - Y_control_pre)
true_effect <- (16 - 12) - (11 - 10)
cat("\nTRUE Treatment Effect (manual DiD):", true_effect, "\n\n")



TRUE Treatment Effect (manual DiD): 3 


The fixed effects regression (Equation 1) gives the same estimate (The fixest package offers a family of functions to perform estimations with multiple fixed-effects):


classic 2x2 by TWFE
traditional_twfe <- fixest::feols(outcome ~ treated | group_id + period_id, data = data)

cat("Traditional TWFE Estimate:", coef(traditional_twfe)["treated"], "\n")


Traditional TWFE Estimate: 3 


Finally, we use the Frisch-Waugh-Lovell (FWL) Theorem to estimate the TWFE model.
Per the FWL Theorem, the TWFE model is equivalent to the residualized regression: yigt−μ̂g−η̂t=τD̃gt+ε̃gty_{igt} - \hat{\mu}_g - \hat{\eta}_t = \tau \tilde{D}_{gt} + \tilde{\varepsilon}_{gt}

starting with yigt=μg+ηt+τgtDgt+εigty_{igt} = \mu_g + \eta_t + \tau_{gt}D_{gt} + \varepsilon_{igt} regress the output yigty_{igt} on fixed effects (say [μg,ηt][\mu_g, \eta_t])

the fitted values are ŷigt=μ̂g+η̂t\hat{y}_{igt} = \hat{\mu}_g + \hat{\eta}_t and so the residuals are ỹigt=yigt−μ̂g−η̂t\tilde{y}_{igt} = y_{igt} - \hat{\mu}_g -\hat{\eta}_t

regress the treatment indicator DgtD_{gt} on fixed effects (say [αg,δt][\alpha_g, \delta_t])

the fitted values are D̂gt\hat{D}_{gt} and so the residuals are D̃gt=Dgt−D̂gt\tilde{D}_{gt} = D_{gt} -\hat{D}_{gt}

regress the output residuals on the treatment residuals ỹigt=τD̃gt+ε̃gt\tilde{y}_{igt} = \tau\tilde{D}_{gt} + \tilde{\varepsilon}_{gt}



Step 1 - Regress outcome on fixed effects
outcome_on_fe <- 
  fixest::feols(outcome ~ 1 | group_id + period_id, data = data)
data$outcome_resid <- resid(outcome_on_fe)






Step 1 - outcome fitted values
outcome_on_fe$fitted.values |>
  matrix(nrow=2, dimnames = list(c("A","B"),c("1","2")))


      1     2
A  9.25 12.75
B 11.75 15.25







Step 1 - outcome fitted residuals
resid(outcome_on_fe) |> 
  matrix(nrow=2, dimnames = list(c("A","B"),c("1","2")))


      1     2
A  0.75 -0.75
B -0.75  0.75




We can now compute the treatment residuals the same way and then regress the outcome residuals on the treatment residuals, per FWL


Step 2 - Regress outcome residuals on treatment residuals
# regress treatment on fixed effects
treatment_on_fe <- 
  fixest::feols(treated ~ 1 | group_id + period_id, data = data)
data$treated_resid <- resid(treatment_on_fe)

# use residuals to estimate TWFE regression
lm(outcome_resid ~ treated_resid, data = data) |> broom::tidy()


# A tibble: 2 × 5
  term          estimate std.error statistic  p.value
  <chr>            <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>
1 (Intercept)   2.08e-17  7.23e-17  2.88e- 1 8.00e- 1
2 treated_resid 3   e+ 0  2.89e-16  1.04e+16 9.28e-33


And we see that the calculation per FWL is the same as computed by the other two estimators. Note that the FWL estimate can be calculated directly using the residuals
βFWL=cov(D̃gt,ỹigt)var(D̃gt)
\beta_{FWL}=\frac{\text{cov}(\tilde{D}_{gt},\tilde{y}_{igt})}{\text{var}(\tilde{D}_{gt})}



variance-covariance calculation
cov(data$treated_resid, data$outcome_resid) /
  var(data$treated_resid)


[1] 3


This is equal to the alternative formulation using the mean of the outcomes and the treatment indicator.
βFWL=cov(D̃gt,ŷigt)cov(D̃gt,Dgt)
\beta_{FWL}=\frac{\text{cov}(\tilde{D}_{gt},\hat{y}_{igt})}{\text{cov}(\tilde{D}_{gt},D_{gt})}



alternate variance-covariance calculation
cov(data$treated_resid, data$outcome_resid) /
  var(data$treated_resid)


[1] 3


alternate variance-covariance calculation
cov(data$treated_resid, data$outcome) /
  cov(data$treated_resid, data$treated)


[1] 3


We’ll now look a little deeper at how the result was arrived at with the FWL estimator, and what it exposes about the structure of the treatment estimator.

title: TwoStageDiD_draft
section: Outcome Residuals in Terms of Potential Outcomes
text:
Outcome Residuals in Terms of Potential Outcomes

Setup: The Data Generating Process
Under the potential outcomes framework, each unit has two potential outcomes: (i) Yigt0Y_{igt}^0 = potential outcome under no treatment, and (ii) Yigt1Y_{igt}^1 = potential outcome under treatment
The observed outcome is: Yigt=Yigt0+Dgt(Yigt1−Yigt0)=Yigt0+Dgtτigt
Y_{igt} = Y_{igt}^0 + D_{gt}(Y_{igt}^1 - Y_{igt}^0) = Y_{igt}^0 + D_{gt}\tau_{igt}

Where τigt=Yigt1−Yigt0\tau_{igt} = Y_{igt}^1 - Y_{igt}^0 is the individual treatment effect.


Parallel Trends Assumption
Under parallel trends, the untreated potential outcomes follow: Yigt0=μg+ηt+εigt
Y_{igt}^0 = \mu_g + \eta_t + \varepsilon_{igt}

Where: μg\mu_g = group fixed effect (time-invariant group characteristics), ηt\eta_t = time fixed effect (group-invariant time shocks), and εigt\varepsilon_{igt} = idiosyncratic error with E[εigt]=0E[\varepsilon_{igt}] = 0


The FWL First Stage: Outcome on Fixed Effects
When we regress outcomes on fixed effects using all observations: Yigt=μ̂g+η̂t+residualigt
Y_{igt} = \hat{\mu}_g + \hat{\eta}_t + \text{residual}_{igt}

The outcome residuals are: Ỹigt=Yigt−μ̂g−η̂t
\tilde{Y}_{igt} = Y_{igt} - \hat{\mu}_g - \hat{\eta}_t



Decomposition by Treatment Status

Case 1: Untreated Observations (Dgt=0D_{gt} = 0)
For untreated units, the observed outcome is: Yigt=Yigt0=μg+ηt+εigt
Y_{igt} = Y_{igt}^0 = \mu_g + \eta_t + \varepsilon_{igt}

Under consistent fixed effects estimation: μ̂g→pμgandη̂t→pηt
\hat{\mu}_g \xrightarrow{p} \mu_g \quad \text{and} \quad \hat{\eta}_t \xrightarrow{p} \eta_t

Therefore, the outcome residual is: Ỹigt=Yigt−μ̂g−η̂t→p(μg+ηt+εigt)−(μg+ηt)=εigt
\tilde{Y}_{igt} = Y_{igt} - \hat{\mu}_g - \hat{\eta}_t \xrightarrow{p} (\mu_g + \eta_t + \varepsilon_{igt}) - (\mu_g + \eta_t) = \varepsilon_{igt}

For untreated units: Ỹigt≈εigt\tilde{Y}_{igt} \approx \varepsilon_{igt} (idiosyncratic error)


Case 2: Treated Observations (Dgt=1D_{gt} = 1)
For treated units, the observed outcome is: Yigt=Yigt1=Yigt0+τigt=μg+ηt+εigt+τigt
Y_{igt} = Y_{igt}^1 = Y_{igt}^0 + \tau_{igt} = \mu_g + \eta_t + \varepsilon_{igt} + \tau_{igt}

The crucial insight is that μ̂g\hat{\mu}_g and η̂t\hat{\eta}_t are estimated from both treated and untreated observations. Under parallel trends, these estimates converge to the counterfactual fixed effects: μ̂g→pμgandη̂t→pηt
\hat{\mu}_g \xrightarrow{p} \mu_g \quad \text{and} \quad \hat{\eta}_t \xrightarrow{p} \eta_t

Therefore, the outcome residual is: Ỹigt=Yigt−μ̂g−η̂t→p(μg+ηt+εigt+τigt)−(μg+ηt)=εigt+τigt
\tilde{Y}_{igt} = Y_{igt} - \hat{\mu}_g - \hat{\eta}_t \xrightarrow{p} (\mu_g + \eta_t + \varepsilon_{igt} + \tau_{igt}) - (\mu_g + \eta_t) = \varepsilon_{igt} + \tau_{igt}

For treated units: Ỹigt≈εigt+τigt\tilde{Y}_{igt} \approx \varepsilon_{igt} + \tau_{igt} (idiosyncratic error + treatment effect)



Cell-Level Averages

Untreated Cells (Dgt=0D_{gt} = 0)
The average outcome residual in an untreated cell is: Ỹ‾gt=1Ngt∑i=1NgtỸigt≈1Ngt∑i=1Ngtεigt→p0
\bar{\tilde{Y}}_{gt} = \frac{1}{N_{gt}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{gt}} \tilde{Y}_{igt} \approx \frac{1}{N_{gt}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{gt}} \varepsilon_{igt} \xrightarrow{p} 0

Untreated cell average: Ỹ‾gt≈0\bar{\tilde{Y}}_{gt} \approx 0


Treated Cells (Dgt=1D_{gt} = 1)
The average outcome residual in a treated cell is:
Ỹ‾gt=1Ngt∑i=1NgtỸigt≈1Ngt∑i=1Ngt(εigt+τigt)
\bar{\tilde{Y}}_{gt} = \frac{1}{N_{gt}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{gt}} \tilde{Y}_{igt} \approx \frac{1}{N_{gt}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{gt}} (\varepsilon_{igt} + \tau_{igt})

=1Ngt∑i=1Ngtεigt+1Ngt∑i=1Ngtτigt→p0+τ‾gt=τ‾gt
= \frac{1}{N_{gt}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{gt}} \varepsilon_{igt} + \frac{1}{N_{gt}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{gt}} \tau_{igt} \xrightarrow{p} 0 + \bar{\tau}_{gt} = \bar{\tau}_{gt}

Where τ‾gt=1Ngt∑i=1Ngtτigt\bar{\tau}_{gt} = \frac{1}{N_{gt}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{gt}} \tau_{igt} is the average treatment effect in cell (g,t)(g,t).
Treated cell average: Ỹ‾gt≈τ‾gt\bar{\tilde{Y}}_{gt} \approx \bar{\tau}_{gt}



Key Insights

1. Residuals Capture Treatment Effects
For treated observations, the outcome residuals capture both: (i) the individual treatment effect τigt\tau_{igt}, and (ii) The idiosyncratic error εigt\varepsilon_{igt}.


2. Cell Averages Isolate Treatment Effects
When we average within cells, the idiosyncratic errors εigt\varepsilon_{igt} cancel out, leaving: - Untreated cells: Ỹ‾gt≈0\bar{\tilde{Y}}_{gt} \approx 0 - Treated cells: Ỹ‾gt≈τ‾gt\bar{\tilde{Y}}_{gt} \approx \bar{\tau}_{gt}.


3. Fixed Effects Provide Counterfactuals
The estimated fixed effects μ̂g+η̂t\hat{\mu}_g + \hat{\eta}_t represent the counterfactual outcome that treated units would have had without treatment (their Yigt0Y_{igt}^0).


4. Parallel Trends is Crucial
This decomposition only works under parallel trends. If parallel trends fails, then:
$$
\hat{\mu}_g + \hat{\eta}_t \not\xrightarrow{p} \mu_g + \eta_t
$$ for treated units, and the residuals won’t cleanly separate treatment effects from errors.

title: TwoStageDiD_draft
text:
Connection to TWFE Estimation
This explains why the TWFE estimate becomes: τ̂TWFE=∑g,twgtỸ‾gt≈∑g,t:Dgt=1wgtτ‾gt
\hat{\tau}_{TWFE} = \sum_{g,t} w_{gt} \bar{\tilde{Y}}_{gt} \approx \sum_{g,t: D_{gt}=1} w_{gt} \bar{\tau}_{gt}

The outcome residuals for treated cells directly estimate the cell-specific average treatment effects τ‾gt\bar{\tau}_{gt}, which is why TWFE becomes a weighted average of these treatment effects.
The problem is that the weights wgtw_{gt} depend on the problematic treatment residuals D̃gt\tilde{D}_{gt}, not on natural sample-size weighting.

title: TwoStageDiD_draft
text:
The TWFE Decomposition

Starting Point: FWL Second-Stage Regression
Starting with the FWL second-stage regression:
ỹigt=τTWFE×D̃gt+ε̃igt
\tilde{y}_{igt} = \tau_{TWFE} \times\tilde{D}_{gt} + \tilde{\varepsilon}_{igt}

The OLS estimator is:
τ̂TWFE=∑i,g,tD̃gtyigt∑i,g,tD̃gtDgt
\hat{\tau}_{TWFE} =\frac{\sum_{i,g,t}\tilde{D}_{gt}y_{igt}}{\sum_{i,g,t}\tilde{D}_{gt}D_{gt}}

We can rewrite this by grouping observations within each (g,t)(g,t) cell where, in general, we have NgtN_{gt} samples:
τ̂TWFE=∑i,g,tD̃gtyigt∑i,g,tD̃gtDgt=∑g,tNgtD̃gt1Ngt∑i=1Ngtyigt∑g,tNgtD̃gt1Ngt∑i=1NgtDgt=∑gt:Dg,t=1NgtD̃gtτ‾gt∑gt:Dg,t=1NgtD̃gt=∑gt:Dg,t=1NgtN1wgtτgt
\begin{align*}
\hat{\tau}_{TWFE} & =\frac{\sum_{i,g,t}\tilde{D}_{gt}y_{igt}}{\sum_{i,g,t}\tilde{D}_{gt}D_{gt}}\\
 & =\frac{\sum_{g,t}N_{gt}\tilde{D}_{gt}\frac{1}{N_{gt}}\sum_{i=1}^{N_{gt}}y_{igt}}{\sum_{g,t}N_{gt}\tilde{D}_{gt}\frac{1}{N_{gt}}\sum_{i=1}^{N_{gt}}D_{gt}}\\
 & =\frac{\sum_{gt:D_{g,t}=1}N_{gt}\tilde{D}_{gt}\bar{\tau}_{gt}}{\sum_{gt:D_{g,t}=1}N_{gt}\tilde{D}_{gt}}\\
 & =\sum_{gt:D_{g,t}=1}\frac{N_{gt}}{N_{1}}w_{gt}\tau_{gt}
\end{align*}



Weighted Average Form
This can be expressed as:
τ̂TWFE=∑gt:Dg,t=1NgtN1wgtτgt
\hat{\tau}_{TWFE} = \sum_{gt:D_{g,t}=1}\frac{N_{gt}}{N_{1}}w_{gt}\tau_{gt}

Where the weights are:
wgt=D̃gt∑gt:Dg,t=1NgtD̃gt
w_{gt}=\frac{\tilde{D}_{gt}}{\sum_{gt:D_{g,t}=1}N_{gt}\tilde{D}_{gt}}

Note: These weights can be negative when D̃gt<0\tilde{D}_{gt} < 0, and they sum to 1:
∑g,twgt=1
\sum_{g,t} w_{gt} = 1

title: TwoStageDiD_draft
text: Negative treatment residuals

title: TwoStageDiD_draft
text:
Goodman-Bacon decomposition
The introduction of differential treatment timing makes it hard to draw a bright line between pre and post treatment periods.
To show this we use the following data:


new data - 3 groups x 10 periods
dat4_rev = data.frame(
  group_id = rep(1:3, times = 10),
  period_id = rep(1:10, each = 3)
) |>
  within({
    treated = (group_id == 2 & period_id >= 5) | (group_id == 3 & period_id >= 8)
    btrue = ifelse(treated & group_id == 3, 4, ifelse(treated & group_id == 2, 2, 0))
    outcome = group_id + 1 * period_id + btrue * treated
  }) |> 
    dplyr::mutate(
    treated = as.numeric(treated)
    , treatment_effect = 
      dplyr::case_when(
        group_id == 2 & period_id >= 5 ~ 2
        , group_id == 3 & period_id >= 8 ~ 4
        , TRUE ~ 0
      )
  )
dat4_rev


   group_id period_id outcome btrue treated treatment_effect
1         1         1       2     0       0                0
2         2         1       3     0       0                0
3         3         1       4     0       0                0
4         1         2       3     0       0                0
5         2         2       4     0       0                0
6         3         2       5     0       0                0
7         1         3       4     0       0                0
8         2         3       5     0       0                0
9         3         3       6     0       0                0
10        1         4       5     0       0                0
11        2         4       6     0       0                0
12        3         4       7     0       0                0
13        1         5       6     0       0                0
14        2         5       9     2       1                2
15        3         5       8     0       0                0
16        1         6       7     0       0                0
17        2         6      10     2       1                2
18        3         6       9     0       0                0
19        1         7       8     0       0                0
20        2         7      11     2       1                2
21        3         7      10     0       0                0
22        1         8       9     0       0                0
23        2         8      12     2       1                2
24        3         8      15     4       1                4
25        1         9      10     0       0                0
26        2         9      13     2       1                2
27        3         9      16     4       1                4
28        1        10      11     0       0                0
29        2        10      14     2       1                2
30        3        10      17     4       1                4




new data plotted
require(ggplot2, quietly = TRUE)
#theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 22))
ggplot(dat4_rev, aes(x = period_id, y = outcome, col = factor(group_id))) +
  geom_point() + geom_line() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(4.5, 7.5), lty = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::pretty_breaks()) +
  labs(x = "Time period", y = "Outcome variable", col = "ID") +
  theme_light(base_size = 18) 









Here we see that our simulation includes two distinct treatment periods. The first treatment occurs at period 5, where group_id=2’s trendline jumps by 2 units. The second treatment occurs in period 8, where group_id=3’s trendline jumps by 4 units. In contrast, id=1 remains untreated for the duration of the experiment.
It’s not immediately clear how to calculate the ATT. For example, how should the late treated unit (group_id=3) regard the early treated unit (group_id=2)? Can the latter be used as control group for the former? After all, they didn’t receive treatment at the same time… but, on the other hand, group_id=2’s path was already altered by the initial treatment wave.
Start by estimating a simple TWFE model (three ways):


TWFE estimated with new data
# (1) using fixest::feols directly
traditional_twfe4_rev <- fixest::feols(outcome ~ treated | group_id + period_id, data = dat4_rev)

# (2) using straight FWL
treatment_on_fe4_rev <- fixest::feols(treated ~ 1 | group_id + period_id, data = dat4_rev)
dat4_rev$treated_resid <- resid(treatment_on_fe4_rev)

outcome_on_fe4_rev <- fixest::feols(outcome ~ 1 | group_id + period_id, data = dat4_rev)
dat4_rev$outcome_resid <- resid(outcome_on_fe4_rev)

treated <- dat4_rev |> dplyr::filter(treated == 1)
# (3) using weights
weights <- treated$treated_resid/sum(treated$treated_resid)

# (3) calculate ATT directly
treated <- dat4_rev |>
  dplyr::filter(treated == 1)
true_ATT <- treated |>
  dplyr::pull(treatment_effect) |> mean()

tibble::tibble(
  "True ATT" = true_ATT
  , "Traditional TWFE Estimate" = coef(traditional_twfe4_rev)[1]
  , "FWL Estimated" = lm(outcome_resid ~ treated_resid, data = dat4_rev) |> 
  broom::tidy() |> dplyr::filter(term == 'treated_resid') |> dplyr::pull(estimate)
  , "Weighted average using TWFE weights" =
     treated$treatment_effect %*% weights
) |> 
  tidyr::pivot_longer(everything()) |> gt::gt('name')









value




True ATT
2.666667


Traditional TWFE Estimate
2.909091


FWL Estimated
2.909091


Weighted average using TWFE weights
2.909091







So all our methods agree to 6 digits, and they are all wrong. What is going on?
The short answer is that it comprises a weighted average of four distinct 2x2 groups (or comparisons):

treated vs untreated 1) early treated (TeTe) vs untreated (UU) 2) late treated (TlTl) vs untreated (UU)
differentially treated 1) early treated (TeTe) vs late control (ClCl) 2) late treated (TlTl) vs early control (CeCe)

We can visualize these four comparison sets as follows:


Goodman-Bacon decomposition plot
rbind(
  dat4_rev |> subset(group_id %in% c(1,2)) |> 
    transform(role = ifelse(group_id==2, "Treatment", "Control"), comp = "1.1. Early vs Untreated"),
  dat4_rev |> subset(group_id %in% c(1,3)) |> 
    transform(role = ifelse(group_id==3, "Treatment", "Control"), comp = "1.2. Late vs Untreated"),
  dat4_rev |> subset(group_id %in% c(2,3) & period_id<8) |> 
    transform(role = ifelse(group_id==2, "Treatment", "Control"), comp = "2.1. Early vs Untreated"),
  dat4_rev |> subset(group_id %in% c(2:3) & period_id>4) |> 
    transform(role = ifelse(group_id==3, "Treatment", "Control"), comp = "2.2. Late vs Untreated")
) |>
  ggplot(aes(period_id, outcome, group = group_id, col = factor(group_id), lty = role)) +
  geom_point() + geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(~comp) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::pretty_breaks()) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Control" = 5, "Treatment" = 1)) +
  labs(x = "Time period", y = "Outcome variable", col = "ID", lty = "Role") +
  theme_light(base_size = 14) 









In other words, the panel group_time IDs are split into different timing cohorts based on when the first treatment takes place and where it lies in relation to the treatment of other panel IDs. The more panel IDs and differential treatment timings there are, the more the combinations of the above groups.
The Goodman-Bacon decomposition reveals a fundamental issue with two-way fixed effects (TWFE) difference-in-differences estimators when treatment timing varies across units: the overall treatment effect estimate is a weighted average of all possible 2×2 DD comparisons, including problematic ones where already-treated units serve as controls for newly-treated units.
The key insight is that TWFE doesn’t just compare treated to never-treated units. When treatment rolls out over time, it also compares “early-treated” to “later-treated” groups. This becomes problematic because already-treated units are poor controls—they’ve already experienced treatment effects, potentially violating the parallel trends assumption for the comparison group.
More troublingly, some of these comparisons can receive negative weights in the overall average. This means that if treatment effects grow over time (dynamic effects are present), or if effects are heterogeneous across groups, TWFE can produce severely biased estimates—even getting the wrong sign. For instance, a beneficial treatment might appear harmful because the negative-weighted comparisons dominate.
The decomposition essentially unpacks the “black box” of TWFE, showing researchers exactly which comparisons drive their estimates and with what weights. This exposed why many DD studies with staggered adoption were likely misestimating treatment effects, spurring the development of new estimators (like Callaway-Sant’Anna, Sun-Abraham, and others) that avoid these problematic comparisons by only using not-yet-treated or never-treated units as controls.
The practical implication: researchers can no longer naively apply TWFE to staggered adoption designs without carefully considering treatment effect dynamics and heterogeneity.

title: TwoStageDiD_draft
text:
The right approach
How to explain the failure of TWFE regression to compute the correct ATT when we have differential treatment timing or heterogeneous effects? One explanation is that the treated values bias the computation of the fixed effects.
Looking at the group-time outcomes ygty_{gt} (since we have just one unit of data per group-time):


show data by group-time
dmnms <- 
  list(
    paste("group",as.character(1:3))
    , as.character(1:10)
    )
dat4_rev |> dplyr::arrange(group_id) |> 
  dplyr::pull(outcome) |> round(2) |> 
  matrix(nrow = 3,byrow =T, dimnames = dmnms) |> 
  as.data.frame() |> 
  knitr::kable("html",digits=2,table.attr = "style='width:40%;'") |> 
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 1, width = "5em") |> 
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 2:11, width = "3em") |> 
  kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = F) |> 
  kableExtra::footnote(footnote_as_chunk=T, general = "group 1 is never treated.")

Outcome data by group-time


By definition fixed effects are constant by group and by time. By convention, the group effects are zero for group-times (1,.) and the time effects are zero for group-times (.,1). Regressing the output against the fixed effects, should estimate the fixed effect, and the residual outcomes should then just be the causal effects, but this does not happen if we use all the outcomes in the regression:


show residuals by group-time using all outcome data
dat4_rev |> dplyr::arrange(group_id) |> 
  dplyr::pull(outcome_resid) |> round(2) |> 
  matrix(nrow = 3,byrow =T, dimnames = dmnms) |> 
  as.data.frame() |> 
  knitr::kable("html", digits=2, table.attr = "style='width:40%;'") |>

Residual outcome data by group-time, estimates using all outomes


However, if we use only the non-treated data to estimate the fixed effects, we get what we expect under the parallel trends assumption: after removing fixed effects, all that is left is treatment effects (easy to see with only one unit per group-time).


show residuals by group-time using only non-treated outcome data
# Stage 1: Estimate fixed effects using ONLY UNTREATED observations
untreated_data4_rev <- dat4_rev[dat4_rev$treated == 0, ]
stage1 <- fixest::feols(outcome ~ 1 | group_id + period_id, data = untreated_data4_rev)
# compute residuals
dat4_rev$outcome_resid_gardner <- 
  dat4_rev$outcome - predict(stage1, newdata = dat4_rev)

dat4_rev |> dplyr::arrange(group_id) |> 
  dplyr::pull(outcome_resid_gardner) |> round(2) |> 
  matrix(nrow = 3,byrow =T, dimnames = dmnms) |> 
  as.data.frame() |> 
  knitr::kable("html",digits=2,table.attr = "style='width:40%;'") |> 
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 1, width = "5em") |> 
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 2:11, width = "3em") |> 
  kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = F) |>

Residual outcome data by group-time, estimates only non-treated outomes


We can now regress these residuals against the treatment indicator and estimate the ATT:


estimating fixed effects with untreated only
# Stage 2: Regress residualized outcome on treatment
stage2 <- fixest::feols(outcome_resid_gardner ~ treated, data = dat4_rev)

cat("Gardner Two-Stage Estimate:", round(coef(stage2)[2], 6), "\n")


Gardner Two-Stage Estimate: 2.666667 


Which is the ATT as constructed in this example.
This two-stage DiD calculation is implemented in the did2s package, which we call as follows:


estimate treatment effects with 2sdid
res <- did2s::did2s(
  data = dat4_rev
  , yname = 'outcome'
  , treatment = 'treated'
  , first_stage = ~ 0 | group_id + period_id
  , second_stage = ~i(treated, ref=FALSE)
  , cluster_var = 'group_id'
  , verbose = FALSE
)
#res
#wsx <- 
  fixest::etable(
  res, fitstat=c('n'), markdown = "images/" #tex = FALSE
) 





and did2s will also prepare an event study plot:


event study using 2sdid
out <- did2s::event_study(
    data = dat4_rev |> 
      dplyr::mutate(
        g = dplyr::case_when(
          group_id == 1 ~ 0
          , group_id == 5 ~ 0
          , TRUE ~ 8
        )
      )
  , yname = 'outcome'
  , idname = 'group_id'
  , gname = 'g'
  , tname = 'period_id'
  , estimator = "did2s"
)
did2s::plot_event_study(out) + 
  theme_light(base_size = 18) + 
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"), legend.position="none")

title: Kalman
text:
Sure, here is an example of how you might use the Kalman filter to predict customer churn in a banking context using R and the tidyverse package. Note that this is a simplified illustration and assumes you have a dataset with relevant variables.

Sample Data
Let’s assume we have a dataset customer_data with the following columns: - customer_id: Unique identifier for each customer. - transaction_freq: Frequency of transactions in the current month. - avg_balance: Average account balance in the current month. - cust_service_calls: Number of customer service interactions in the current month. - churn: Whether the customer churned (1) or not (0).


Sample Code
# Load necessary libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(KFAS)

# Generate sample data
set.seed(123)
customer_data <- tibble(
  customer_id = 1:100,
  transaction_freq = rnorm(100, mean = 10, sd = 2),
  avg_balance = rnorm(100, mean = 5000, sd = 1000),
  cust_service_calls = rpois(100, lambda = 2),
  churn = sample(c(0, 1), 100, replace = TRUE)
)

# Define the state space model
# For simplicity, we'll use a local level model for each state variable
# Assume normal distribution for observations

# Build a state space model
build_model <- function(data) {
  ssmodel <- SSModel(
    H = matrix(NA, 3, 3),
    Q = diag(NA, 3),
    Z = diag(3),
    T = diag(3),
    R = diag(3),
    a1 = c(mean(data$transaction_freq), mean(data$avg_balance), mean(data$cust_service_calls)),
    P1 = diag(NA, 3)
  )
  
  ssmodel$y <- t(as.matrix(data %>% select(transaction_freq, avg_balance, cust_service_calls)))
  return(ssmodel)
}

# Fit the model
fit_model <- function(ssmodel) {
  fit <- fitSSM(ssmodel, inits = rep(0, 6), method = "BFGS")
  return(fit$model)
}

# Kalman filter application
apply_kalman_filter <- function(model) {
  kf <- KFS(model, filtering = "mean", smoothing = "mean")
  return(kf)
}

# Predict churn probabilities based on filtered states
predict_churn <- function(kf, threshold = 0.5) {
  smoothed_states <- kf$a
  churn_prob <- 1 / (1 + exp(-rowMeans(smoothed_states))) # Logistic function for churn probability
  churn_pred <- ifelse(churn_prob > threshold, 1, 0)
  return(churn_pred)
}

# Build, fit, and apply the Kalman filter model
ssmodel <- build_model(customer_data)
fitted_model <- fit_model(ssmodel)
kf_results <- apply_kalman_filter(fitted_model)

# Predict churn
customer_data <- customer_data %>%
  mutate(predicted_churn = predict_churn(kf_results))

# Display results
print(customer_data)

# Evaluate the model
confusion_matrix <- table(customer_data$churn, customer_data$predicted_churn)
print(confusion_matrix)
accuracy <- sum(diag(confusion_matrix)) / sum(confusion_matrix)
print(paste("Accuracy:", accuracy))


Explanation:

Data Generation: Simulate customer data for the example.
State Space Model Definition: Define a simple state space model where each state variable is modeled independently.
Model Fitting: Fit the state space model using the Kalman filter.
Filtering and Smoothing: Apply the Kalman filter and smoother to estimate the hidden states.
Churn Prediction: Use the smoothed states to predict churn probabilities, applying a logistic function.
Evaluation: Compare the predicted churn with the actual churn to evaluate model performance.

This code provides a simplified illustration. In a real-world application, you would need to preprocess the data, handle missing values, and potentially use more complex state space models to capture the dynamics of customer behavior more accurately.






Note



Please cite KFAS in publications by using: 

Jouni Helske (2017). KFAS: Exponential Family State Space Models in R. Journal of Statistical Software, 78(10), 1-39. doi:10.18637/jss.v078.i10.



library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(KFAS)

# Generate sample data
set.seed(123)
customer_data <- tibble(
  customer_id = 1:100,
  transaction_freq = rnorm(100, mean = 10, sd = 2),
  avg_balance = rnorm(100, mean = 5000, sd = 1000),
  cust_service_calls = rpois(100, lambda = 2),
  churn = sample(c(0, 1), 100, replace = TRUE)
)

customer_data


# Define the state space model
# For simplicity, we'll use a local level model for each state variable
# Assume normal distribution for observations

# Build a state space model
# build_model <- function(data) {
#   ssmodel <- KFAS::SSModel(
#     H = matrix(NA, 3, 3),
#     Q = diag(NA, 3),
#     Z = diag(3),
#     T = diag(3),
#     R = diag(3),
#     a1 = c(mean(data$transaction_freq), mean(data$avg_balance), mean(data$cust_service_calls)),
#     P1 = diag(NA, 3)
#   )
#   
#   ssmodel$y <- t(as.matrix(data %>% select(transaction_freq, avg_balance, cust_service_calls)))
#   return(ssmodel)
# }

build_model <- function(data) {
  # Define the state space model using a formula
  model <- SSModel(
    transaction_freq + avg_balance + cust_service_calls ~ 
      SSMtrend(1, Q = list(NA)),  # Local level model for each variable
    H = matrix(NA, 3, 3),  # Observation noise covariance matrix
    data = data
  )
  
  # Assign observed data to the model
  model$y <- t(as.matrix(data %>% select(transaction_freq, avg_balance, cust_service_calls)))
  
  return(model)
}

# Fit the model
fit_model <- function(ssmodel) {
  fit <- fitSSM(ssmodel, inits = rep(0, 6), method = "BFGS")
  return(fit$model)
}

# Kalman filter application
apply_kalman_filter <- function(model) {
  kf <- KFS(model, filtering = "mean", smoothing = "mean")
  return(kf)
}

# Predict churn probabilities based on filtered states
predict_churn <- function(kf, threshold = 0.5) {
  smoothed_states <- kf$a
  churn_prob <- 1 / (1 + exp(-rowMeans(smoothed_states))) # Logistic function for churn probability
  churn_pred <- ifelse(churn_prob > threshold, 1, 0)
  return(churn_pred)
}


# Build, fit, and apply the Kalman filter model
ssmodel <- build_model(customer_data)
fitted_model <- fit_model(ssmodel)
kf_results <- apply_kalman_filter(fitted_model)

# Predict churn
customer_data <- customer_data %>%
  mutate(predicted_churn = predict_churn(kf_results))

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Conditional independence (unconfoundedness): {Yi(0),Yi(1)}⟂Di|Xi=x,for all x∈𝒳\{Y_i(0), Y_i(1)\} \perp D_i | X_i = x, \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathcal{X}
More general form: {Yi(0),Yi(1)}⟂Di|Xi\{Y_i(0), Y_i(1)\} \perp D_i | X_i



τ(x)=𝔼[Yi(1)−Yi(0)|Xi=x]\tau(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) | X_i = x]



Stratified estimator: τ̂STRAT=∑x∈𝒳nxnτ̂(x)\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \frac{n_x}{n} \hat{\tau}(x)
where: τ̂(x)=1nx1∑{Xi=x,Di=1}Yi−1nx0∑{Xi=x,Di=0}Yi\hat{\tau}(x) = \frac{1}{n_{x1}} \sum_{\{X_i=x, D_i=1\}} Y_i - \frac{1}{n_{x0}} \sum_{\{X_i=x, D_i=0\}} Y_i
so
τ̂STRAT=∑x∈𝒳nxnτ̂(x)=∑x∈𝒳nxn(1nx1∑{Xi=x,Di=1}Yi−1nx0∑{Xi=x,Di=0}Yi)=1n∑x∈𝒳(nxnx1∑{Xi=x,Di=1}Yi−nxnx0∑{Xi=x,Di=0}Yi)=1n∑x∈𝒳(1πx∑{Xi=x,Di=1}Yi−11−πx∑{Xi=x,Di=0}Yi)
\begin{align*}
\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} & =\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\frac{n_{x}}{n}\hat{\tau}(x)\\
 & =\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\frac{n_{x}}{n}\left(\frac{1}{n_{x1}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=1\}}Y_{i}-\frac{1}{n_{x0}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=0\}}Y_{i}\right)\\
 & =\frac{1}{n}\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\left(\frac{n_{x}}{n_{x1}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=1\}}Y_{i}-\frac{n_{x}}{n_{x0}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=0\}}Y_{i}\right)\\
 & =\frac{1}{n}\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\left(\frac{1}{\pi_{x}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=1\}}Y_{i}-\frac{1}{1-\pi_{x}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=0\}}Y_{i}\right)
\end{align*}

Notation: (i) nx=|{i:Xi=x}|n_x = |\{i : X_i = x\}| , (ii) nxw=|{i:Xi=x,Di=w}|n_{xw} = |\{i : X_i = x, D_i = w\}|



Central limit theorem for stratified estimator: n(τ̂STRAT−τ)⇒N(0,VSTRAT)\sqrt{n}(\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} - \tau) \Rightarrow N(0, V_{STRAT})
Asymptotic variance: VSTRAT=Var[τ(Xi)]+𝔼[σ(1)2(Xi)e(Xi)+σ(0)2(Xi)1−e(Xi)]V_{STRAT} = \text{Var}[\tau(X_i)] + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{\sigma^2_{(1)}(X_i)}{e(X_i)} + \frac{\sigma^2_{(0)}(X_i)}{1-e(X_i)}\right]
where: - e(x)=ℙ[Di=1|Xi=x]e(x) = \mathbb{P}[D_i = 1 | X_i = x] (propensity score) - σ(w)2(x)=Var[Yi(w)|Xi=x]\sigma^2_{(w)}(x) = \text{Var}[Y_i^{(w)} | X_i = x]



Definition of propensity score: e(x)=ℙ[Di=1|Xi=x]e(x) = \mathbb{P}[D_i = 1 | X_i = x]
Balancing property: {Yi(0),Yi(1)}⟂Di|e(Xi)\{Y_i(0), Y_i(1)\} \perp D_i | e(X_i)
Proof of balancing property: ℙ[Di=w|{Yi(w)},e(Xi)]=∫𝒳ℙ[Di=w|{Yi(w)},Xi=x]ℙ[Xi=x|{Yi(w)},e(Xi)]dx=∫𝒳ℙ[Di=w|Xi=x]ℙ[Xi=x|{Yi(w)},e(Xi)]dx(unconf.)={e(Xi)if w=11−e(Xi)else\begin{align}
\mathbb{P}[D_i = w | \{Y_i(w)\}, e(X_i)] &= \int_{\mathcal{X}} \mathbb{P}[D_i = w | \{Y_i(w)\}, X_i = x] \mathbb{P}[X_i = x | \{Y_i(w)\}, e(X_i)] dx \\
&= \int_{\mathcal{X}} \mathbb{P}[D_i = w | X_i = x] \mathbb{P}[X_i = x | \{Y_i(w)\}, e(X_i)] dx \quad \text{(unconf.)} \\
&= \begin{cases}
e(X_i) & \text{if } w = 1 \\
1 - e(X_i) & \text{else}
\end{cases}
\end{align}



Sorting step: ê(Xi1)≤ê(Xi2)≤…≤ê(Xin)\hat{e}(X_{i_1}) \leq \hat{e}(X_{i_2}) \leq \ldots \leq \hat{e}(X_{i_n})
Stratum-specific estimator: τ̂j=∑j=⌊(j−1)n/J⌋+1⌊jn/J⌋DiYi∑j=⌊(j−1)n/J⌋+1⌊jn/J⌋Di−∑j=⌊(j−1)n/J⌋+1⌊jn/J⌋(1−Di)Yi∑j=⌊(j−1)n/J⌋+1⌊jn/J⌋(1−Di)\hat{\tau}_j = \frac{\sum_{j=\lfloor(j-1)n/J\rfloor+1}^{\lfloor jn/J\rfloor} D_i Y_i}{\sum_{j=\lfloor(j-1)n/J\rfloor+1}^{\lfloor jn/J\rfloor} D_i} - \frac{\sum_{j=\lfloor(j-1)n/J\rfloor+1}^{\lfloor jn/J\rfloor} (1-D_i) Y_i}{\sum_{j=\lfloor(j-1)n/J\rfloor+1}^{\lfloor jn/J\rfloor} (1-D_i)}
Propensity stratification estimator: τ̂PSTRAT=1J∑j=1Jτ̂j\hat{\tau}_{PSTRAT} = \frac{1}{J} \sum_{j=1}^J \hat{\tau}_j



IPW estimator with estimated propensity scores: τ̂IPW=1n∑i=1n(DiYiê(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−ê(Xi))\hat{\tau}_{IPW} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{D_i Y_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right)
Oracle IPW estimator (true propensity scores): τ̂IPW*=1n∑i=1n(DiYie(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−e(Xi))\hat{\tau}^*_{IPW} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{D_i Y_i}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-e(X_i)}\right)



Central limit theorem for oracle IPW: n(τ̂IPW*−τ)⇒N(0,VIPW*)\sqrt{n}(\hat{\tau}^*_{IPW} - \tau) \Rightarrow N(0, V_{IPW^*})
Asymptotic variance: VIPW*=Var[τ(Xi)]+𝔼[(μ(0)(Xi)+(1−e(Xi))τ(Xi))2e(Xi)(1−e(Xi))]+𝔼[σ(1)2(Xi)e(Xi)+σ(0)2(Xi)1−e(Xi)]V_{IPW^*} = \text{Var}[\tau(X_i)] + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{(\mu_{(0)}(X_i) + (1-e(X_i))\tau(X_i))^2}{e(X_i)(1-e(X_i))}\right] + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{\sigma^2_{(1)}(X_i)}{e(X_i)} + \frac{\sigma^2_{(0)}(X_i)}{1-e(X_i)}\right]
Alternative decomposition in proof: DiYie(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−e(Xi)=τ(Xi)+(Die(Xi)−1−Di1−e(Xi))c(Xi)+Diεi(1)e(Xi)−(1−Di)εi(0)1−e(Xi)\frac{D_i Y_i}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-e(X_i)} = \tau(X_i) + \left(\frac{D_i}{e(X_i)} - \frac{1-D_i}{1-e(X_i)}\right) c(X_i) + \frac{D_i \varepsilon_i^{(1)}}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) \varepsilon_i^{(0)}}{1-e(X_i)}
where: - c(x)=μ(0)(x)+(1−e(x))τ(x)c(x) = \mu_{(0)}(x) + (1-e(x))\tau(x) - εi(w)=Yi(w)−μ(w)(Xi)\varepsilon_i^{(w)} = Y_i^{(w)} - \mu_{(w)}(X_i)



Relationship between IPW and stratified variances: VIPW*=VSTRAT+𝔼[(μ(0)(Xi)+(1−e(Xi))τ(Xi))2e(Xi)(1−e(Xi))]V_{IPW^*} = V_{STRAT} + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{(\mu_{(0)}(X_i) + (1-e(X_i))\tau(X_i))^2}{e(X_i)(1-e(X_i))}\right]
Therefore: VIPW*≥VSTRATV_{IPW^*} \geq V_{STRAT} (IPW is generally less efficient than stratification)



Weak overlap: 𝔼[1/(e(Xi)(1−e(Xi)))]<∞\mathbb{E}[1/(e(X_i)(1-e(X_i)))] < \infty
Strong overlap: η≤e(x)≤1−ηfor all x∈𝒳\eta \leq e(x) \leq 1-\eta \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathcal{X}
for some η>0\eta > 0.



When 𝒳\mathcal{X} is discrete, the stratified estimator can be written as IPW: τ̂STRAT=1n∑i=1n(DiYiê(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−ê(Xi))\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{D_i Y_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right)
where: ê(x)=nx1nx\hat{e}(x) = \frac{n_{x1}}{n_x}



Conditional mean functions: μ(w)(x)=𝔼[Yi(w)|Xi=x]\mu^{(w)}(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y_i^{(w)} | X_i = x]
Conditional variance functions: σ(w)2(x)=Var[Yi(w)|Xi=x]\sigma^{(w)2}(x) = \text{Var}[Y_i^{(w)} | X_i = x]
Sample sizes by stratum and treatment: - nx=|{i:Xi=x}|n_x = |\{i : X_i = x\}| (total in stratum xx) - nx1=|{i:Xi=x,Di=1}|n_{x1} = |\{i : X_i = x, D_i = 1\}| (treated in stratum xx)
- nx0=|{i:Xi=x,Di=0}|n_{x0} = |\{i : X_i = x, D_i = 0\}| (control in stratum xx)



Decomposition of stratified estimator: τ̂STRAT=∑x∈𝒳λ(x)τ(x)+∑x∈𝒳(λ̂(x)−λ(x))τ(x)+∑x∈𝒳λ(x)(τ̂(x)−τ(x))+∑x∈𝒳(λ̂(x)−λ(x))(τ̂(x)−τ(x))\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \lambda(x)\tau(x) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} (\hat{\lambda}(x) - \lambda(x)) \tau(x) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \lambda(x) (\hat{\tau}(x) - \tau(x)) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} (\hat{\lambda}(x) - \lambda(x)) (\hat{\tau}(x) - \tau(x))
where: - λ(x)=ℙ[Xi=x]\lambda(x) = \mathbb{P}[X_i = x] (true probability) - λ̂(x)=nx/n\hat{\lambda}(x) = n_x/n (empirical probability)



Palo Alto treatment effect calculation: τ̂PA=5152+5−1222362+122≈−1.7%\hat{\tau}_{PA} = \frac{5}{152 + 5} - \frac{122}{2362 + 122} \approx -1.7\%
Geneva treatment effect calculation: τ̂GVA=350350+581−19792278+1979≈−8.9%\hat{\tau}_{GVA} = \frac{350}{350 + 581} - \frac{1979}{2278 + 1979} \approx -8.9\%
Combined estimate: τ̂=26412641+5188τ̂PA+51882641+5188τ̂GVA≈−6.5%\hat{\tau} = \frac{2641}{2641 + 5188} \hat{\tau}_{PA} + \frac{5188}{2641 + 5188} \hat{\tau}_{GVA} \approx -6.5\%

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Conditional independence (unconfoundedness): {Yi(0),Yi(1)}⟂Di|Xi=x,for all x∈𝒳\{Y_i(0), Y_i(1)\} \perp D_i | X_i = x, \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathcal{X}
More general form: {Yi(0),Yi(1)}⟂Di|Xi\{Y_i(0), Y_i(1)\} \perp D_i | X_i

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text: τ(x)=𝔼[Yi(1)−Yi(0)|Xi=x]\tau(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) | X_i = x]

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Stratified estimator: τ̂STRAT=∑x∈𝒳nxnτ̂(x)\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \frac{n_x}{n} \hat{\tau}(x)
where: τ̂(x)=1nx1∑{Xi=x,Di=1}Yi−1nx0∑{Xi=x,Di=0}Yi\hat{\tau}(x) = \frac{1}{n_{x1}} \sum_{\{X_i=x, D_i=1\}} Y_i - \frac{1}{n_{x0}} \sum_{\{X_i=x, D_i=0\}} Y_i
so
τ̂STRAT=∑x∈𝒳nxnτ̂(x)=∑x∈𝒳nxn(1nx1∑{Xi=x,Di=1}Yi−1nx0∑{Xi=x,Di=0}Yi)=1n∑x∈𝒳(nxnx1∑{Xi=x,Di=1}Yi−nxnx0∑{Xi=x,Di=0}Yi)=1n∑x∈𝒳(1πx∑{Xi=x,Di=1}Yi−11−πx∑{Xi=x,Di=0}Yi)
\begin{align*}
\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} & =\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\frac{n_{x}}{n}\hat{\tau}(x)\\
 & =\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\frac{n_{x}}{n}\left(\frac{1}{n_{x1}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=1\}}Y_{i}-\frac{1}{n_{x0}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=0\}}Y_{i}\right)\\
 & =\frac{1}{n}\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\left(\frac{n_{x}}{n_{x1}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=1\}}Y_{i}-\frac{n_{x}}{n_{x0}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=0\}}Y_{i}\right)\\
 & =\frac{1}{n}\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\left(\frac{1}{\pi_{x}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=1\}}Y_{i}-\frac{1}{1-\pi_{x}}\sum_{\{X_{i}=x,D_{i}=0\}}Y_{i}\right)
\end{align*}

Notation: (i) nx=|{i:Xi=x}|n_x = |\{i : X_i = x\}| , (ii) nxw=|{i:Xi=x,Di=w}|n_{xw} = |\{i : X_i = x, D_i = w\}|

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Central limit theorem for stratified estimator: n(τ̂STRAT−τ)⇒N(0,VSTRAT)\sqrt{n}(\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} - \tau) \Rightarrow N(0, V_{STRAT})
Asymptotic variance: VSTRAT=Var[τ(Xi)]+𝔼[σ(1)2(Xi)e(Xi)+σ(0)2(Xi)1−e(Xi)]V_{STRAT} = \text{Var}[\tau(X_i)] + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{\sigma^2_{(1)}(X_i)}{e(X_i)} + \frac{\sigma^2_{(0)}(X_i)}{1-e(X_i)}\right]
where: - e(x)=ℙ[Di=1|Xi=x]e(x) = \mathbb{P}[D_i = 1 | X_i = x] (propensity score) - σ(w)2(x)=Var[Yi(w)|Xi=x]\sigma^2_{(w)}(x) = \text{Var}[Y_i^{(w)} | X_i = x]

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
IPW estimator with estimated propensity scores: τ̂IPW=1n∑i=1n(DiYiê(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−ê(Xi))\hat{\tau}_{IPW} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{D_i Y_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right)
Oracle IPW estimator (true propensity scores): τ̂IPW*=1n∑i=1n(DiYie(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−e(Xi))\hat{\tau}^*_{IPW} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{D_i Y_i}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-e(X_i)}\right)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Central limit theorem for oracle IPW: n(τ̂IPW*−τ)⇒N(0,VIPW*)\sqrt{n}(\hat{\tau}^*_{IPW} - \tau) \Rightarrow N(0, V_{IPW^*})
Asymptotic variance: VIPW*=Var[τ(Xi)]+𝔼[(μ(0)(Xi)+(1−e(Xi))τ(Xi))2e(Xi)(1−e(Xi))]+𝔼[σ(1)2(Xi)e(Xi)+σ(0)2(Xi)1−e(Xi)]V_{IPW^*} = \text{Var}[\tau(X_i)] + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{(\mu_{(0)}(X_i) + (1-e(X_i))\tau(X_i))^2}{e(X_i)(1-e(X_i))}\right] + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{\sigma^2_{(1)}(X_i)}{e(X_i)} + \frac{\sigma^2_{(0)}(X_i)}{1-e(X_i)}\right]
Alternative decomposition in proof: DiYie(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−e(Xi)=τ(Xi)+(Die(Xi)−1−Di1−e(Xi))c(Xi)+Diεi(1)e(Xi)−(1−Di)εi(0)1−e(Xi)\frac{D_i Y_i}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-e(X_i)} = \tau(X_i) + \left(\frac{D_i}{e(X_i)} - \frac{1-D_i}{1-e(X_i)}\right) c(X_i) + \frac{D_i \varepsilon_i^{(1)}}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) \varepsilon_i^{(0)}}{1-e(X_i)}
where: - c(x)=μ(0)(x)+(1−e(x))τ(x)c(x) = \mu_{(0)}(x) + (1-e(x))\tau(x) - εi(w)=Yi(w)−μ(w)(Xi)\varepsilon_i^{(w)} = Y_i^{(w)} - \mu_{(w)}(X_i)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Relationship between IPW and stratified variances: VIPW*=VSTRAT+𝔼[(μ(0)(Xi)+(1−e(Xi))τ(Xi))2e(Xi)(1−e(Xi))]V_{IPW^*} = V_{STRAT} + \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{(\mu_{(0)}(X_i) + (1-e(X_i))\tau(X_i))^2}{e(X_i)(1-e(X_i))}\right]
Therefore: VIPW*≥VSTRATV_{IPW^*} \geq V_{STRAT} (IPW is generally less efficient than stratification)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
When 𝒳\mathcal{X} is discrete, the stratified estimator can be written as IPW: τ̂STRAT=1n∑i=1n(DiYiê(Xi)−(1−Di)Yi1−ê(Xi))\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{D_i Y_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-D_i) Y_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right)
where: ê(x)=nx1nx\hat{e}(x) = \frac{n_{x1}}{n_x}

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Conditional mean functions: μ(w)(x)=𝔼[Yi(w)|Xi=x]\mu^{(w)}(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y_i^{(w)} | X_i = x]
Conditional variance functions: σ(w)2(x)=Var[Yi(w)|Xi=x]\sigma^{(w)2}(x) = \text{Var}[Y_i^{(w)} | X_i = x]
Sample sizes by stratum and treatment: - nx=|{i:Xi=x}|n_x = |\{i : X_i = x\}| (total in stratum xx) - nx1=|{i:Xi=x,Di=1}|n_{x1} = |\{i : X_i = x, D_i = 1\}| (treated in stratum xx)
- nx0=|{i:Xi=x,Di=0}|n_{x0} = |\{i : X_i = x, D_i = 0\}| (control in stratum xx)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Decomposition of stratified estimator: τ̂STRAT=∑x∈𝒳λ(x)τ(x)+∑x∈𝒳(λ̂(x)−λ(x))τ(x)+∑x∈𝒳λ(x)(τ̂(x)−τ(x))+∑x∈𝒳(λ̂(x)−λ(x))(τ̂(x)−τ(x))\hat{\tau}_{STRAT} = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \lambda(x)\tau(x) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} (\hat{\lambda}(x) - \lambda(x)) \tau(x) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \lambda(x) (\hat{\tau}(x) - \tau(x)) + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} (\hat{\lambda}(x) - \lambda(x)) (\hat{\tau}(x) - \tau(x))
where: - λ(x)=ℙ[Xi=x]\lambda(x) = \mathbb{P}[X_i = x] (true probability) - λ̂(x)=nx/n\hat{\lambda}(x) = n_x/n (empirical probability)

title: Unconfoundedness and Propensity Scores
text:
Palo Alto treatment effect calculation: τ̂PA=5152+5−1222362+122≈−1.7%\hat{\tau}_{PA} = \frac{5}{152 + 5} - \frac{122}{2362 + 122} \approx -1.7\%
Geneva treatment effect calculation: τ̂GVA=350350+581−19792278+1979≈−8.9%\hat{\tau}_{GVA} = \frac{350}{350 + 581} - \frac{1979}{2278 + 1979} \approx -8.9\%
Combined estimate: τ̂=26412641+5188τ̂PA+51882641+5188τ̂GVA≈−6.5%\hat{\tau} = \frac{2641}{2641 + 5188} \hat{\tau}_{PA} + \frac{5188}{2641 + 5188} \hat{\tau}_{GVA} \approx -6.5\%

title: RCTs to Regression
text:
Treatment indicator: Di∈0,1D_i\in {0,1}

example: eligibility for expanded Medicaid

Outcome: YiY_{i}

example: number of doctor visits in past 6 months

Potential outcomes Yi0,Yi1Y_{i}^{0},Y_{i}^{1}
Individual-level treatment effect ΔiY=Yi1−Yi0\Delta_i^Y=Y_i^1-Y_i^0 (can never know this).
Unbiased estimate of average treatment effect:
ΔY=𝔼[Yi1−Yi0]
\Delta^{Y}=\mathbb{E}\left[Y_{i}^{1}-Y_{i}^{0}\right]

or OLS coefficient on 𝐷𝑖 from this regression:
Yi=α+δDi+ϵiY_{i}=\alpha+\delta D_{i}+\epsilon_{i}

url_str  <- 
  'https://github.com/Mixtape-Sessions/Machine-Learning/blob/main/Labs/data/'
file_str <- 
  'oregon_hie_table5.csv?raw=true'
  
dat <- readr::read_csv(
  paste0(url_str, file_str, show_col_types = FALSE)
) %>% 
  dplyr::select(doc_num, treatment, weight, dplyr::starts_with("ddd")) %>% 
  tidyr::drop_na()


fit <- lm(doc_num ~ treatment, data = dat, weights = dat$weight)

effect <- fit %>% 
  broom::tidy() %>% 
  dplyr::filter(term == 'treatment') %>% 
  dplyr::pull(estimate)

stringr::str_glue(
  "Estimated effect of Medicaid eligibility on 
  number of doctor visits: {scales::number(effect, accuracy = 0.001)}"
)

Estimated effect of Medicaid eligibility on 
number of doctor visits: 0.268





The bivariate regression above leans heavily on random assignment of treatment:
Di⟂⟂Yi0,Yi1D_{i}\perp\!\!\!\!\perp Y_{i}^{0},Y_{i}^{1}
Sometimes, even in an RCT, treatment is assigned randomly only conditional on some set of covariates XiX_i.

example: in the Oregon HIE, eligibility for Medicaid was granted via lottery, but households with more members could have more lottery entries. So the lottery outcome is random only conditional on household size.

So what happens if we don’t have random assignment? In terms of our regression model above, it means ϵi\epsilon_i may be correlated with DiD_i. For example, perhaps household size, XiX_i, which increases the probability of treatment, is also associated with more doctor visits. If XiX_i is omitted from the model, it is buried in the error term:
ϵi=βXi+ηi
\epsilon_{i}=\beta X_{i}+\eta_{i}
 We’ll assume for now that everything else related to doctor visits (ηi\eta_i) is unrelated to treatment. What does our bivariate regression coefficient deliver in this case?
δ̂OLS=Cov(Yi,Di)Var(Di)=δ+βCov(Xi,Di)Var(Di)(1)\hat{\delta}^{\text{OLS}}=\frac{Cov\left(Y_{i},D_{i}\right)}{\text{Var}\left(D_{i}\right)}=\delta+\beta\frac{Cov\left(X_{i},D_{i}\right)}{\text{Var}\left(D_{i}\right)}
 \qquad(1)
Simple regression gives us what we want (δ\delta) plus an omitted variables bias term. The form of this term tells us what kinds of XiX_i variables we should take care to control for in our regressions.
According to the ommitted variable bias (OVB) formula (Equation 1), what kinds of variables should you be be sure to control for in regressions?
Careful investigators will find a set of regressors XiX_i for which they are willing to assume treatment is as good as randomly assigned:
Di⟂⟂(Yi(0),Yi(1))|Xi.
D_i\perp\!\!\!\!\perp\left( Y_{i}\left( 0\right) ,Y_{i}\left( 1\right) \right) |X_{i}
\text{.}
 This combined with a linear model for the conditional expectation of Yi0Y_{i}^0 and Yi1Y_{i}^1 given XiX_{i} means we can estimate the average treatment via OLS on the following regression equation:
Yi=δDi+Xi′β+εi.
Y_{i}=\delta D_{i}+X_{i}^{\prime }\beta +\varepsilon _{i}.


# Add the household size indicators to our regressor set and run regression:
fit <- lm(doc_num ~ ., data = dat %>% dplyr::select(-weight), weights = dat$weight)
effect <- fit %>% 
  broom::tidy() %>% 
  dplyr::filter(term == 'treatment') %>% 
  dplyr::pull(estimate)
stringr::str_glue(
  "Estimated effect of Medicaid eligibility on 
  number of doctor visits (with controls): {scales::number(effect, accuracy = 0.001)}"
)

Estimated effect of Medicaid eligibility on 
number of doctor visits (with controls): 0.314


How did the estimate of the effect of Medicaid eligiblity change? What does that tell us about the relationship between the included regressors and the outcome and treatment?



Where does machine learning fit into this? It might be tempting to treat this regression as a prediction exercise where we are predicting YiY_{i} given DiD_{i} and XiX_{i}. Don’t give in to this temptation. We are not after a prediction for YiY_{i}, we are after a coefficient on DiD_{i}.
Modern machine learning algorithms are finely tuned for producing predictions, but along the way they compromise coefficients. So how can we deploy machine learning in the service of estimating the causal coefficient δ\delta?
To see where ML fits in, first remember that an equivalent way to estimate δ\delta is the following three-step procedure:

Regress YiY_{i} on XiX_{i} and compute the residuals, Ỹi=Yi−ŶiOLS\tilde{Y}_{i}=Y_{i}-\hat{Y}_{i}^{OLS}, where ŶiOLS=Xi′(X′X)−1X′Y\hat{Y}_{i}^{OLS}=X_{i}^{\prime}\left( X^{\prime }X\right) ^{-1}X^{\prime }Y
Regress DiD_{i} on XiX_{i} and compute the residuals, D̃i=Di−D̂iOLS\tilde{D}_{i}=D_{i}-\hat{D}_{i}^{OLS}, where D̂iOLS=Xi′(X′X)−1X′D\hat{D}_{i}^{OLS}=X_{i}^{\prime}\left( X^{\prime }X\right) ^{-1}X^{\prime }D
Regress Ỹi\tilde{Y}_{i} on D̃i\tilde{D}_{i}.

Let’s try it!

# Regress outcome on covariates (not treatment)
yreg <- lm(
  doc_num ~ .
  , data = dat %>% dplyr::select(doc_num, dplyr::starts_with("ddd"))
  , weights = dat$weight
)
# Calculate residuals
ytilde = yreg$residuals

# regress treatment on covariates (not outcome)
dreg <- lm(
  treatment ~ .
  , data = dat %>% dplyr::select(treatment, dplyr::starts_with("ddd"))
  , weights = dat$weight
)
# Calculate residuals
dtilde = dreg$residuals

# regress ytilde on dtilde
fit <- lm(
  ytilde ~ dtilde
  , data = tibble::tibble(ytilde = ytilde, dtilde = dtilde)
  , weights = dat$weight)

effect <- fit %>% 
  broom::tidy() %>% 
  dplyr::filter(term == 'dtilde') %>% 
  dplyr::pull(estimate)

stringr::str_glue(
  "Estimated effect of Medicaid eligibility on 
  number of doctor visits (partialled out): {scales::number(effect, accuracy = 0.001)}"
)

Estimated effect of Medicaid eligibility on 
number of doctor visits (partialled out): 0.314


ML enters the picture by providing an alternate way to generate Ŷi\hat{Y}_i and D̂i\hat{D}_i when OLS is not the best tool for the job. The first two steps are really just prediction exercises, and in principle any supervised machine learning algorithm can step in here.
Back to the whiteboard!
